(a) the **`IsRatHilb` rationality machinery** — a predicate `IsRatHilb f d` ("`f : ℕ → ℚ` is the
eventual-coefficient function of a rational series `p·(1−X)^{−d}`") plus its closure lemmas, used to
run the Stacks 00K1 induction inside `lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational`; and (b) two
**schematic-support predicate helpers** for `Scheme.Modules`. Add a concise blueprint entry for each.

## Required content
Add one concise block per Lean declaration below. Read `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean`
for each exact signature. These are **project-internal, Archon-original** helpers — no external
`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block required (the `IsRatHilb` closure lemmas realize standard steps of the
Stacks 00K1 inductive argument; a one-line `\textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}` pointer is optional on
the predicate `def:` but not required).

Rationality machinery (namespace `AlgebraicGeometry`, names `IsRatHilb...`):
- `AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb` (definition, `Prop`) — `IsRatHilb f d` holds iff the function
  `f : ℕ → ℚ` agrees, for all `n`, with the `n`-th coefficient of some rational power series
  `p(X)·(1−X)^{−d}` with `p ∈ ℚ[X]` (read the def for the exact formulation). This is the
  "is a numerical-polynomial-eventually / rational Hilbert series" predicate the induction tracks.
- `AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero` — a function eventually zero (`f n = 0` for `n > N`)
  is `IsRatHilb f d` for any `d` (it equals a polynomial times `(1−X)^{−d}` with that polynomial the
  finite sum). Base case of the induction.
- `AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump` — if `IsRatHilb f d` then `IsRatHilb f (d+1)` (raise the pole
  order by multiplying numerator by `(1−X)`).
- `AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub` — `IsRatHilb` is closed under pointwise subtraction at fixed `d`.
- `AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight` — closure under the right shift `n ↦ f (n−1)`
  (multiplication of the series by `X`).
- `AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff` — the **anti-difference / summation** step: if the
  difference sequence is `IsRatHilb` (pole order `e`) and agrees beyond `N`, then the summed
  sequence `H` is `IsRatHilb` with pole order `e+1`. This is the inductive heart (a finite
  difference raises the pole order by one).
- `AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq` — packaging: if `hM − hC + hK(·−1)` telescopes
  appropriately (read the statement), `hM` is `IsRatHilb`. The form used to apply the SES degree
  recursion.
- `AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul` — coefficient identity for
  `invOneSubPow` (the formal series `(1−X)^{−d}`) multiplied by `X` / a degree-one factor; the
  power-series computation behind `shiftRight`/`bump`. One-to-two lines.
- `AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff` — the power-series-level lemma underlying
  `IsRatHilb.antidiff` (the partial-sum series equals numerator over `(1−X)^{e+1}`). One-to-two lines.

Schematic-support predicate helpers (namespace `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules`):
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le` — over an affine open `U`, the

- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupportι` (definition) — the closed immersion
  `schematicSupport F ⟶ X` of the schematic support into the ambient scheme. Supports
  `def:schematic_support` / `def:has_proper_support`.

For each: short statement in project notation, `\label{}` (suggest `lem:ratHilb_*` / `def:ratHilb`

109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110

112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}

117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}

126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism

135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. 205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:

210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;

212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function

118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.

122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing

128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.

237	\begin{lemma}
238	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
239	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
241	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
242	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
243	  % Polynomials" (read from
244	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
245	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
246	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
247	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
248	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
249	  %  the function
250	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
251	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
252	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
253	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
254	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
255	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
256	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
257	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
258	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
259	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
260	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
261	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
262	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
263	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
264	\end{lemma}
265	
266	\begin{proof}
267	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
268	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
269	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
270	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
271	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
272	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
273	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
274	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
275	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
276	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
277	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
278	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
279	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
280	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
281	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
282	\end{proof}

303	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the

390	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
391	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
392	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
393	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
394	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
395	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
396	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
397	  such that for all \(n > N\)
398	  \[
399	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
400	  \]
401	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
402	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
403	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
404	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
405	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).

408	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
409	  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
410	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
411	  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
412	  rational series from the module.

431	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
432	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
433	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
434	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
435	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
436	%  following short exact sequence
437	%  $$
438	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
439	%  $$
440	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
441	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
442	\begin{proof}
443	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
444	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
445	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
446	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
447	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
448	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
449	
450	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
451	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
452	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
453	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
454	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
455	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). Then \(R\) is a graded
456	  quotient of a polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) with the \(x_i\) of
457	  degree \(1\); we induct on the minimal number \(r\) of degree-one generators.
458	
459	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} Here \(R = \kappa\) and \(M\) is a finitely
460	  generated \(\kappa\)-module, hence finite-dimensional, so \(M_n = 0\) and
461	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\). The Hilbert series
462	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is therefore a polynomial; taking \(d = 0\)
463	  and \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) the polynomial with these coefficients gives
464	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{0}\bigr)\) for all \(n\).
465	
466	  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(x = x_r \in R_1\) be one of a minimal set of
467	  degree-one generators, so that the induction hypothesis applies to finitely
468	  generated graded modules over \(R/(x)\), itself a graded quotient of
469	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_{r-1}]\). Multiplication by \(x\) raises degree by one
470	  and assembles, for every \(n\), into a short exact sequence of
471	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector spaces
472	  \[
473	    0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{\;x\;} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0,
474	  \]
475	  where \(K = \ker\bigl(x : M \to M(1)\bigr)\) and \(C = M / xM\) are finitely
476	  generated graded \(R\)-modules annihilated by \(x\), hence finitely generated
477	  graded modules over \(R/(x)\). By additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact
478	  sequence (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}, applied to the kernel and
479	  cokernel of the map \(x\)),
480	  \[
481	    \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
482	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n
483	  \]
484	  for every \(n\). Multiplying the Hilbert series of \(M\) by \((1 - X)\) and
485	  summing this identity against \(X^{n}\) shows that
486	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) equals the Hilbert series of
487	  \(C\) minus that of \(K\), up to a polynomial in low degrees. By the induction
488	  hypothesis applied to the \(R/(x)\)-modules \(K\) and \(C\) (which live over
489	  \(r - 1\) variables), each of those Hilbert series is, up to a polynomial, of
490	  the form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\) with \(d - 1 \le r - 1\). Hence
491	  \[
492	    (1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n
493	      \;=\; q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)} \qquad \text{up to a polynomial,}
494	  \]
495	  and multiplying by the inverse power series
496	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1) = (1 - X)^{-1}\)
497	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) gives
498	  \[
499	    \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n
500	      \;=\; p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \qquad \text{up to a polynomial,}
501	  \]
502	  with \(d \le r\). Reading off the coefficient of \(X^n\) for \(n \gg 0\) yields
503	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) with
504	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\),
505	  which is the claim.
506	\end{proof}

508	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
509	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
510	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
511	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
512	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
513	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
514	  % Polynomials" (read from
515	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
516	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
517	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
518	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
519	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
520	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
521	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
522	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
523	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
524	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
525	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
526	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
527	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
528	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
529	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
530	  \[
531	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
532	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
533	  \]
534	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
535	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
536	\end{theorem}
537	
538	\begin{proof}
539	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
540	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
541	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
542	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
543	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
544	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
545	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
546	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
547	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
548	  function
549	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
550	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
551	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
552	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
553	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
554	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
555	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
556	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
557	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
558	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
559	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
560	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
561	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
562	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
563	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
564	\end{proof}

566	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
567	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
568	
569	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
570	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
571	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
572	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
573	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.

575	\begin{definition}

579	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}

590	  % Polynomials" (read from
591	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).

595	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
596	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."

599	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
600	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
601	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
602	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
603	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
604	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
605	  \(U \subseteq X\),

607	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
608	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
609	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),

612	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is

616	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
617	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic

642	\begin{lemma}\leanok
643	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
644	  localization]
645	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
646	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
647	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
648	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
649	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
650	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
651	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
652	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
653	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
654	  \[
655	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
656	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
657	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
658	  \]
659	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
660	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
661	
662	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
663	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
664	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
665	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
666	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
667	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
668	\end{lemma}
669	
670	\begin{proof}
671	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
672	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
673	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
674	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
675	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
676	
677	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
678	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
679	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
680	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
681	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
682	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
683	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
684	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
685	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
686	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
687	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
688	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
689	\end{proof}

1113	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
1114	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
1115	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
1116	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
1117	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
1118	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
1119	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
1120	\begin{proof}
1121	  
1122	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
1123	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
1124	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
1125	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
1126	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
1127	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
1128	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
1129	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
1130	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
1131	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
1132	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
1133	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
1134	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
1135	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
1136	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
1137	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
1138	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
1139	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
1140	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
1141	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
1142	
1143	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
1144	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
1145	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
1146	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
1147	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
1148	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
1149	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
1150	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
1151	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
1152	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
1153	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
1154	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
1155	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
1156	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
1157	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
1158	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
1159	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
1160	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
1161	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
1162	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
1163	\end{proof}

346	namespace Module
347	
348	/-- For a finitely generated module `M` over a commutative ring `R`, the
349	annihilator commutes with localization: if `Rₚ` localizes `R` at a submonoid
350	`S` and `f : M →ₗ[R] Mₚ` localizes `M` at `S`, then the annihilator of `Mₚ`
351	over `Rₚ` is the extension (`Ideal.map` along `algebraMap R Rₚ`) of the
352	annihilator of `M` over `R`.
353	
354	This is the abstract `IsLocalization`/`IsLocalizedModule` form, matching the
355	shape needed for the affine-basic-open coherence of the annihilator ideal sheaf
356	(`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator`, `def:modules_annihilator`):
357	the structure-sheaf restriction `Γ(X,U) → Γ(X, D(f))` is
358	`IsLocalization (powers f)`, and for a quasi-coherent `F` the section
359	restriction is `IsLocalizedModule (powers f)`.
360	
361	Mathlib has no annihilator-localization lemma, so this is project-local. -/
362	theorem annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map
363	    {R : Type*} [CommRing R] (S : Submonoid R)
364	    {Rₚ : Type*} [CommRing Rₚ] [Algebra R Rₚ] [IsLocalization S Rₚ]
365	    {M : Type*} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [Module.Finite R M]
366	    {Mₚ : Type*} [AddCommGroup Mₚ] [Module R Mₚ] [Module Rₚ Mₚ] [IsScalarTower R Rₚ Mₚ]
367	    (f : M →ₗ[R] Mₚ) [IsLocalizedModule S f] :
368	    Module.annihilator Rₚ Mₚ = (Module.annihilator R M).map (algebraMap R Rₚ) := by
369	  classical
370	  obtain ⟨t, htop⟩ := (Module.Finite.fg_top (R := R) (M := M))
371	  -- annihilating a spanning finset suffices for membership in the annihilator
372	  have key : ∀ (r : R), (∀ m ∈ t, r • m = 0) → r ∈ Module.annihilator R M := by
373	    intro r h
374	    rw [Module.mem_annihilator]
375	    intro x
376	    have hx : x ∈ Submodule.span R (t : Set M) := htop ▸ Submodule.mem_top
377	    induction hx using Submodule.span_induction with
378	    | mem y hy => exact h y hy
379	    | zero => simp
380	    | add a b _ _ ha hb => rw [smul_add, ha, hb, add_zero]
381	    | smul c a _ ha => rw [smul_comm, ha, smul_zero]
382	  apply le_antisymm
383	  · -- `Ann Rₚ Mₚ ⊆ (Ann R M).map`: clear one common denominator over the generators
384	    intro y hy
385	    rw [Module.mem_annihilator] at hy
386	    obtain ⟨⟨a, s⟩, rfl⟩ := IsLocalization.mk'_surjective S y
387	    dsimp only at hy ⊢
388	    have hgen : ∀ m ∈ t, ∃ u : S, (u : R) • a • m = 0 := by
389	      intro m hm
390	      have hz := hy (IsLocalizedModule.mk' f m (1 : S))
391	      rw [IsLocalizedModule.mk'_smul_mk' Rₚ f, IsLocalizedModule.mk'_eq_zero,
392	        IsLocalizedModule.eq_zero_iff S f] at hz
393	      obtain ⟨u, hu⟩ := hz
394	      exact ⟨u, hu⟩
395	    choose u hu using hgen
396	    obtain ⟨U, hU⟩ : ∃ U : S, ∀ m ∈ t, (U : R) • a • m = 0 := by
397	      refine ⟨∏ x ∈ t.attach, u x.1 x.2, ?_⟩
398	      intro m hm
399	      obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ :=
400	        Finset.dvd_prod_of_mem (fun x : t => u x.1 x.2) (Finset.mem_attach t ⟨m, hm⟩)
401	      have hcoe : ((∏ x ∈ t.attach, u x.1 x.2 : S) : R) = (u m hm : R) * (c : R) := by
402	        rw [hc]; push_cast; ring
403	      rw [hcoe, mul_smul, smul_comm, hu m hm, smul_zero]
404	    have hUa : (U : R) * a ∈ Module.annihilator R M := by
405	      apply key; intro m hm; rw [mul_smul]; exact hU m hm
406	    have heq : IsLocalization.mk' Rₚ a s
407	        = (algebraMap R Rₚ ((U : R) * a)) * IsLocalization.mk' Rₚ 1 (U * s) := by
408	      rw [← IsLocalization.mk'_eq_mul_mk'_one, IsLocalization.mk'_eq_iff_eq]
409	      push_cast; ring
410	    rw [heq]
411	    exact Ideal.mul_mem_right _ _ (Ideal.mem_map_of_mem _ hUa)
412	  · -- `(Ann R M).map ⊆ Ann Rₚ Mₚ`: the image of an annihilator annihilates
413	    rw [Ideal.map_le_iff_le_comap]
414	    intro a ha
415	    rw [Ideal.mem_comap, Module.mem_annihilator]
416	    rw [Module.mem_annihilator] at ha
417	    intro x
418	    obtain ⟨⟨m, s⟩, rfl⟩ := IsLocalizedModule.mk'_surjective S f x
419	    dsimp only [Function.uncurry]
420	    rw [← IsLocalization.mk'_one (M := S) Rₚ a, IsLocalizedModule.mk'_smul_mk' Rₚ f, ha m,

452	    (hδ : ∀ n, N < n → δ n = ((q : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ e).val).coeff n)
453	    (hH : ∀ n, N < n → H (n + 1) - H n = δ (n + 1)) :
454	    ∃ (p : Polynomial ℚ), ∀ n, N < n →
455	      H n = ((p : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ (e + 1)).val).coeff n := by
456	  set F : ℚ⟦X⟧ := (q : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ e).val with hF
457	  -- Partial-sum identity: the order-`(e+1)` series accumulates the order-`e` coefficients.
458	  have hsum : ∀ m, ((q : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ (e + 1)).val).coeff m
459	      = ∑ k ∈ Finset.range (m + 1), F.coeff k := by
460	    intro m
461	    have hmul : (q : ℚ⟦X⟧) * (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ (e + 1)).val
462	        = (PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ 1).val * F := by
463	      rw [hF, show (e + 1) = 1 + e from by omega, PowerSeries.invOneSubPow_add, Units.val_mul]

468	    intro n hn

470	  have htel : ∀ j, H (N + 1 + j)
471	      = H (N + 1) + ∑ i ∈ Finset.range j, F.coeff (N + 2 + i) := by
472	    intro j

475	    | succ j ih =>
476	        rw [Finset.sum_range_succ, show N + 2 + j = N + 1 + (j + 1) from by omega]
477	        have hs := hstep (N + 1 + j) (by omega)
478	        rw [show (N + 1 + j) + 1 = N + 1 + (j + 1) from by omega] at hs
479	        linarith [hs, ih]

\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
\label{subsec:isRatHilb}

The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).

\begin{lemma}[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
  \(F\):
  \[
    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
  \]
  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
  the Cauchy product.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
  \[
    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
  \]
  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
  \[
    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
  \]
  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Rational Hilbert function]
  \label{def:ratHilb}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
  \[
    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
  \]
  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
  \uses{def:ratHilb}
  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Raising the pole order]
  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Closure under difference]
  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
  \uses{def:ratHilb}
  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Closure under right shift]
  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
  \uses{def:ratHilb}
  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
  Lean.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Antidifference step for the predicate]
  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
  \[
    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
  \]
  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
  \(U\):
  \[
    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
  \]
  This is the always-available (\(\mathrm{ofIdeals}\)) inclusion direction of the
  affine-local characterisation of \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse
  inclusion is the basic-open coherence statement gated on
  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly in Lean from the
  defining \(\mathrm{ofIdeals}\) bound. It supports the well-definedness of the
  schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
\end{lemma}

\begin{definition}[Schematic-support immersion]
  \label{def:schematic_support_immersion}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupportι}
  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:modules_annihilator}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
  \[
    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \;\longhookrightarrow\; X
  \]
  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) into the ambient
  scheme, namely the subscheme immersion of the annihilator ideal sheaf
  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}). It is a
  quasi-compact preimmersion onto the support, and it is the morphism whose
  composite with a base morphism \(f : X \to S\) defines proper support
  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.
\end{definition}
